D3 Chemistry L4 Analyze Reference

Mordant Chelation — Same Dye, Different Metal, Different Colour

a metal ion bridges fibre and dye in a five-membered chelate ring; the same madder root extract becomes pink with Al³⁺, purple with Fe³⁺, brown with Cr³⁺

A mordant is a metal salt — alum, ferrous sulphate, copper sulphate, potassium dichromate — pre-bonded to the fibre. The metal ion M^n+ then forms a five-membered chelate ring with the dye (two oxygen donor atoms from adjacent –OH or =O groups). This locks the dye and shifts its colour through ligand-field effects on the metal.

Toggle the mordant. The chelate-ring schematic redraws with the chosen metal; the swatch on the right shows the historical madder-root colour for that mordant; the lightfastness bar predicts how the dyed cloth survives sunlight.
